messages.content: Which limits are breached (if any), what immediate operational restrictions must be applied, and by what deadline must the bank complete full identification (and what happens if it doesn’t)? Include how you’d handle the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere.

## Applicability (regulatory context)
The limits and restrictions below apply specifically to **“Accounts opened using Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC, in non-face-to-face mode”** under paragraph 25. These are **separate from** (and excluded from) the Enhanced Due Diligence framework for other non-face-to-face onboarding modes, which explicitly applies to “non-face-to-face customer onboarding (other than customer onboarding in terms of paragraph 25)”. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, pp.29-30]; [F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47]

## 1) Limits to test for breach (and what constitutes a breach)
For accounts opened using **Aadhaar OTP-based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode**, the bank must ensure the following quantitative caps are not exceeded:

### A. Deposit accounts (aggregate across *all* deposit accounts of the customer)
- **Aggregate balance cap:** “**The aggregate balance of all the deposit accounts of the customer shall not exceed Rupees One Lakh**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach trigger:** the moment aggregate balance exceeds **₹1,00,000**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- **Aggregate credits cap (financial year):** “**The aggregate of all credits in a financial year, in all the deposit accounts taken together, shall not exceed Rupees Two Lakh**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach trigger:** credits exceeding **₹2,00,000** in the FY (across all deposit accounts taken together). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. Borrowal accounts
- **Product restriction:** “**As regards borrowal accounts, the bank shall sanction only term loans**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- **Aggregate term loan cap (per year):** “**The aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach trigger:** total term loans sanctioned in a year exceeding **₹60,000**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### C. Time cap on operating under OTP-based e-KYC without full identification
- “**The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP).**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach trigger:** continuing operations beyond **one year** from opening without completing identification as required. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 2) Immediate operational restrictions to apply upon breach (what the bank must do right away)
### A. If the **₹1,00,000 aggregate balance** cap is breached
- The regulation is explicit on the immediate control: “**In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD as mentioned at (6) below.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - Operationally, this means the bank must **stop operations** on the account(s) until identification/CDD is completed as permitted under paragraph 25(6) (paragraph 23 identification or V-CIP). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. If the **one-year operating period** is about to end (or has ended) without full identification
- The bank must ensure accounts opened using OTP-based e-KYC **do not operate beyond one year** unless full identification is completed as specified. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### C. If full identification is **not completed within one year** (explicit consequences)
Paragraph 25(7) prescribes mandatory outcomes:
- “**If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure as mentioned above within a year; (a) in respect of deposit accounts, the bank shall close the same immediately, and (b) in respect of borrowal accounts, the bank shall allow no further debits.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Deposit accounts:** must be **closed immediately**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Borrowal accounts:** must permit **“no further debits”**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 3) Deadline for completing “full identification” (CDD) and what it must comprise
### A. Deadline
- The bank must complete identification so that the OTP-based e-KYC account is not allowed “**to operate for more than one year**” without such identification. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. What qualifies as completing identification (CDD) for this purpose
Paragraph 25(6) allows two routes:
- Identification “**as per paragraph 23**”, or [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- Identification “**as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP)**”. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

Under paragraph 23 (individual CDD at onboarding / identification), the bank shall obtain, inter alia:
- Aadhaar number in specified cases / voluntarily, or proof of possession of Aadhaar (with offline verification where possible) or OVD / equivalent e-document, or KYC Identifier with consent to download from CKYCR; **and** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- “**PAN or the equivalent e-document thereof or Form No. 60** …” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]

Where the bank completes identification using V-CIP with Aadhaar details, paragraph 25(6) further requires: “**If the bank uses Aadhaar details under paragraphs 26 and 27, it shall follow the process in its entirety, including fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 4) Handling the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere
Paragraph 25(8) imposes two compliance actions:

### A. Obtain the customer declaration
- “**The bank shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-to-face mode with any other RE.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. CKYCR upload flagging to prevent reuse by other REs
- “**Further, while uploading KYC information to CKYCR, bank shall clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC and other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 5) Additional immediate controls that must be in place for OTP-based e-KYC non-face-to-face accounts (operational guardrails)
Even where no limit is breached yet, paragraph 25 requires the bank to implement the following operational controls (relevant to how you run the account day-to-day):
- **Consent:** “**The Customer shall give specific consent for the authentication through OTP.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- **Transaction alerts/OTPs only to Aadhaar-registered mobile:** the bank must send “**transaction alerts, OTP, etc., only to the mobile number of the customer registered with Aadhaar**”, with a “**Board-approved policy**” for due diligence on mobile number change requests for such accounts. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.29]
- **Monitoring:** “**The bank shall have strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation, to ensure compliance with the above-mentioned conditions.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## What is missing to answer “which limits are breached (if any)” for your case
To determine **which limits are breached in your specific scenario**, the following facts are required but not provided in the query:
- Customer’s **current aggregate balance across all deposit accounts** (to test ₹1,00,000 cap) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- Customer’s **aggregate credits in the current financial year across all deposit accounts** (to test ₹2,00,000 cap) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- Whether the customer has any **borrowal account**, whether it is a **term loan**, and **aggregate term loans sanctioned in the year** (to test ₹60,000 cap) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- The **account opening date** (to compute the one-year operating deadline) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

Which limits are breached (if any), what immediate operational restrictions must be applied, and by what deadline must the bank complete full identification (and what happens if it doesn’t)? Include how you’d handle the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere.

## 1) Limits that can be breached (and when they are treated as breached)

### A. Deposit accounts opened using **Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode**
The following quantitative limits apply:
- **Aggregate balance cap (across all the customer’s deposit accounts): ₹1,00,000**. The text provides: “**The aggregate balance of all the deposit accounts of the customer shall not exceed Rupees One Lakh. In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD as mentioned at (6) below.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- **Aggregate credits cap (financial year; across all deposit accounts taken together): ₹2,00,000**: “**The aggregate of all credits in a financial year, in all the deposit accounts taken together, shall not exceed Rupees Two Lakh.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### B. Borrowal accounts opened using **Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode**
- Product restriction: “**the bank shall sanction only term loans**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- Annual cap: “**The aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### C. Time-based limit (CDD completion deadline trigger)
- The account (deposit and borrowal) must not continue to operate beyond a time limit without full identification: “**The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP).**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

Because no transaction/balance figures (or account age) are provided in the query, I cannot determine whether any limit has *actually* been breached in your case. The regulation does, however, specify exactly which thresholds—₹1,00,000 balance; ₹2,00,000 FY credits; ₹60,000 term loans/year; and the one-year CDD deadline—would constitute a breach when exceeded/not met. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

---

## 2) Immediate operational restrictions the bank must apply

### A. If the **₹1,00,000 aggregate balance threshold** is exceeded
- Immediate restriction is mandatory: “**the bank shall cease the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD as mentioned at (6) below.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### B. If **one year elapses** without completing identification (CDD) per the required method
- The bank “**shall not allow**” such accounts “**to operate for more than one year**” unless identification is completed as required. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- If the bank still does not complete CDD within that year, the regulation prescribes the following immediate outcomes:
  - Deposit accounts: “**the bank shall close the same immediately**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
  - Borrowal accounts: “**the bank shall allow no further debits**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### C. Additional operational control requirement (risk-mitigation) relevant for day-to-day operations
- The bank must ensure transaction alerts/OTPs go only to the Aadhaar-registered mobile number: “**the bank shall ensure that it sends transaction alerts, OTP, etc., only to the mobile number of the customer registered with Aadhaar.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.29), p.29]
- The bank must have a “**Board-approved policy**” for mobile-number change requests in such accounts. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.29), p.29]

### D. Monitoring expectation
- The bank must have “**strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation**” to ensure compliance with these conditions. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

---

## 3) Deadline to complete full identification (CDD) and what happens if the bank doesn’t

### Deadline
- The operative deadline is **within one year** from opening for accounts opened using OTP-based e-KYC, because: “**The bank shall not allow accounts…to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP).**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### Acceptable completion routes (as stated in the extracted text)
- Identification must be completed either:
  - “**as per paragraph 23**” (the content of paragraph 23 itself is not included in the retrieved sections), or
  - “**as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP)**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### If Aadhaar details are used under V-CIP
- Additional mandatory step: “**If the bank uses Aadhaar details under paragraphs 26 and 27, it shall follow the process in its entirety, including fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### Consequence if CDD is not completed within one year
- Deposit accounts: “**close the same immediately**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- Borrowal accounts: “**allow no further debits**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

Therefore, I cannot restate the full identification steps under paragraph 23 or the complete V-CIP process beyond the cited excerpts. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

## 4) Handling the required customer declaration (no OTP-based accounts elsewhere)

### What the bank must obtain from the customer
- The bank must obtain a declaration “**to the effect that no other account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-to-face mode with any other RE.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### What the bank must do when uploading to CKYCR
- “**while uploading KYC information to CKYCR, bank shall clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- And it states the resulting restriction on reuse: “**other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### Operationally (within what is explicitly stated)
- Ensure the declaration is collected as part of the OTP-based e-KYC account opening documentation/process (the regulation mandates obtaining it, but does not prescribe the exact format in the retrieved text). [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- Ensure the CKYCR upload is explicitly flagged as “opened using OTP based e-KYC” so that the restriction on other REs’ reliance is supported by accurate reporting. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

## 5) (Optional comparison) If the account were instead opened as a “Small Account”
If a bank opens a “Small Account,” it comes with different limits, including:
- Annual credits ≤ “**Rupees One Lakh**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.35), p.35]
- Monthly withdrawals/transfers ≤ “**₹10,000**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.35), p.35]
- Balance at any time ≤ “**₹50,000**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.35), p.35]
And the bank must ensure these limits “**are not breached, before it allows a transaction to take place.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.35), p.35]

(Your query appears focused on OTP-based e-KYC accounts; the above is provided only because the retrieved sections also include the Small Account framework.) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.35), p.35]

## 1) Applicable regulatory construct
The limits / restrictions and the one-year deadline you are asking about arise **specifically for “Accounts opened using Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC, in non-face-to-face mode”** and the conditions imposed on such accounts. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.29]

## 2) Limits to test for breach (and what constitutes a breach)
For accounts opened using **Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode**, the bank must ensure compliance with all of the following caps:

### A. Deposit-side caps
- **Aggregate balance cap (all deposit accounts of the customer, taken together):** must **“not exceed Rupees One Lakh.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.29-p.30]
  - **Breach condition:** if the customer’s aggregate balance across deposit accounts exceeds **₹1,00,000**. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.29-p.30]
- **Aggregate credits cap (financial year; all deposit accounts taken together):** must **“not exceed Rupees Two Lakh.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach condition:** if aggregate credits in a financial year exceed **₹2,00,000** across all deposit accounts. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. Borrowal-side caps
- **Product restriction:** **“the bank shall sanction only term loans.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- **Annual sanction cap:** **“The aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach condition:** if term loans sanctioned in a year aggregate beyond **₹60,000**, or if a non-term-loan borrowal facility is sanctioned. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 3) Immediate operational restrictions when limits are breached (or other mandated controls)
### A. Immediate restriction when the ₹1,00,000 aggregate balance cap is breached
- The regulation is explicit that **“In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD”** (as referenced in the same paragraph set). [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - Operationally, this requires **stopping operations immediately** once the aggregate balance crosses **₹1,00,000**, and keeping the account non-operational **until CDD is completed** (per the identification modes described below). [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. Mobile-number / OTP controls that must be enforced for these accounts
Even without a “breach,” OTP-based e-KYC accounts have a mandatory control:
- The bank must ensure it sends **“transaction alerts, OTP, etc., only to the mobile number of the customer registered with Aadhaar.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.29]
- The bank must have a **“Board-approved policy delineating a robust process of due diligence for dealing with requests for change of mobile number in such accounts.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.29; Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]

### C. What about breach of the ₹2,00,000 annual credits cap / ₹60,000 term-loan cap?
The provided text clearly states the caps (₹2,00,000 credits in a financial year; ₹60,000 term loans in a year), but **does not specify a precise immediate “cease operations” action for those breaches** (unlike the explicit cease-operation consequence for the ₹1,00,000 balance breach). [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
What *is* explicitly required is that the bank have **“strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation, to ensure compliance”** with the conditions. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 4) Deadline to complete full identification / CDD, and consequences if not completed
### A. Deadline (hard stop at one year)
- The bank **“shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP).”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- If the bank uses Aadhaar details under V-CIP, it must follow that process **“in its entirety, including fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. What happens if the bank does not complete full CDD within one year?
If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure within one year:
- **Deposit accounts:** **“the bank shall close the same immediately”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- **Borrowal accounts:** **“the bank shall allow no further debits.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 5) How to complete “full identification” (the permitted modes referenced)
The one-year rule requires identification either:

### A. Identification “as per paragraph 23”
Paragraph 23 sets out what the bank must obtain from an individual for CDD while establishing an account-based relationship, including:
- Aadhaar / proof of Aadhaar / OVD / equivalent e-document / CKYCR KYC Identifier (as applicable) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- **“PAN or the equivalent e-document thereof or Form No. 60”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]

### B. Or identification through V-CIP (paragraphs 26 and 27)
The regulation also allows completion of CDD through V-CIP, including for **“new customer onboarding for individual customers”**. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
Also, RBI reiterates that compliant V-CIP is to be treated as face-to-face for these Directions: **“the bank shall treat processes complying with prescribed standards and procedures for V-CIP on par with face-to-face CIP.”** [Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47]

## 6) Handling the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere
For OTP-based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode, the bank must:
- **“obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-to-face mode with any other RE.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- Additionally, when uploading KYC to CKYCR, the bank must **“clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC”** and **“other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face-to-face mode.”** [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 7) Closely related operational governance requirements
- The bank must have monitoring systems and alerts for violations of the OTP e-KYC conditions. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- The bank’s KYC policy is Board-approved and must include “Customer Identification Procedures (CIP)” and “Monitoring of Transactions.” [Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]
- A bank must “not undertake a transaction or commence an account-based relationship without following the CDD procedure.” [Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]

Therefore, I can only list the breach tests and the mandated actions triggered by each threshold; I cannot confirm whether a breach has occurred without the scenario values. ### Deposit-side limits

### Whether limits are breached (if any)

  - Any non-term-loan sanction → breach [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
  - Aggregate term loans sanctioned in a year > **₹60,000** → breach [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### A) If aggregate deposit balance exceeds ₹1,00,000

  - This restriction is specifically triggered when the **balance exceeds** the ₹1,00,000 threshold. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### B) If full identification / CDD is not completed within 1 year
- The bank “shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP).” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

  - **Deposit accounts:** “the bank shall close the same immediately” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
  - **Borrowal accounts:** “the bank shall allow no further debits.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### C) Monitoring / controls expectation
- The bank “shall have strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation, to ensure compliance with the above-mentioned conditions.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

## 3) Deadline to complete full identification (CDD) and what happens if it doesn’t
### Deadline
- For OTP-based e-KYC non-face-to-face accounts, the bank must complete identification so that the account does not operate “for more than **one year** unless it carries out identification as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP).” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### What counts as “full identification” here (as per retrieved text)
- The identification must be **“as per paragraph 23”** (CDD for individuals) or **“as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP)”**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- Paragraph 23 specifies what the bank “shall obtain… from an individual” for undertaking CDD while establishing an account-based relationship (e.g., Aadhaar number / proof of possession of Aadhaar / OVD or equivalent e-document; KYC Identifier with consent for CKYCR download; and PAN/equivalent e-document or Form 60). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.25), p.25]
- If the bank uses Aadhaar details under V-CIP (paragraphs 26 and 27), “it shall follow the process in its entirety, including **fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### Consequence if not completed by the 1-year deadline
- If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure within a year:
  - Deposit accounts must be “close[d]… immediately” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
  - For borrowal accounts, “no further debits” are permitted. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

## 4) Handling the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere
For OTP-based e-KYC non-face-to-face accounts, the bank must do all of the following:

- **Obtain a customer declaration:** “The bank shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-to-face mode with any other RE.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

- **CKYCR tagging / indicator:** “Further, while uploading KYC information to CKYCR, bank shall clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC…” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

- **Downstream restriction for other REs (as stated):** “…and other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

## 5) Regulatory categorisation note (V-CIP)
- Where V-CIP is used for identification, RBI reiterates that the bank shall “treat processes complying with prescribed standards and procedures for V-CIP **on par with face-to-face CIP** for the purpose of these Directions.” [F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence (pp.45-47), pp.45-47]

## Information not available in the retrieved sections (needed to answer fully for your specific case)
- Your actual account figures (current aggregate balance, aggregate credits in the financial year, and term-loan sanctions in the year) are not provided, so I cannot confirm which limits are breached in fact—only the applicable thresholds and consequences are stated in the retrieved text. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- The retrieved text references “paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP)” but does not include paragraph 27 content; therefore, only the quoted obligations around using Aadhaar under V-CIP (including “fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication”) can be stated from the provided pages. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

messages.content: Show the latest Board-approved KYC policy and map each of the 4 mandatory elements (Customer Acceptance, Risk Management, CIP, Monitoring) to operating procedures, systems, and MI.

## 1) What the **latest (current) Board‑approved KYC Policy** must contain under RBI Directions 2025

### 1.1 Board approval & mandatory elements
- The bank **“shall have a KYC policy”** and **“[t]he Board of Directors … or any committee to which the Board has delegated power, shall duly approve the KYC policy.”** [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]
- The KYC policy **“shall include [the] following four key elements”**: **(i) Customer Acceptance Policy; (ii) Risk Management; (iii) Customer Identification Procedures (CIP); and (iv) Monitoring of Transactions.** [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]
- The KYC policy shall **inter alia** include items such as **“Periodic updation of KYC”**, exceptional measures for KYC updation (recent photograph / physical presence / more frequent schedule), obtaining OVD/deemed OVD for change of address during updation, and facility for KYC updation **“at any branch.”** [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]

### 1.2 Additional Board‑approved policies expressly required alongside the KYC framework
- A **Board‑approved policy** detailing **“a robust due diligence process for managing requests to change the registered Mobile Number for Accounts opened in non-face-to-face mode.”** [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]
- A policy approved by the Board (or a committee headed by Chairman/CEO/MD) to establish parameters for **cross‑border correspondent banking**, with **post facto Board (or empowered committee) approval** for new relationships. [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]

> **Limitation (what is missing from the retrieved sections):** The RBI Directions require the bank to have a Board‑approved KYC policy and prescribe what it must contain, but the retrieved sections do **not** include *your bank’s actual Board‑approved policy document text/version/date*. Therefore, I can map RBI‑mandated elements to operating procedures/systems/MI that the policy must cover, but I cannot “show” your bank’s internal policy content from these extracts. [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]

---

## 2) Mapping the 4 mandatory KYC policy elements to Operating Procedures, Systems, and MI (Management Information)

### A) **Customer Acceptance Policy (CAP)**

**Regulatory requirements that must be embedded in CAP**
- The bank **“shall frame a Customer Acceptance Policy.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
- CAP must include (minimum):
  - **“not open any account in an anonymous or fictitious / benami name.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - **“open no account where it is unable to apply appropriate CDD measures”** due to non‑cooperation / unreliability of documents; and **“consider filing an STR, if necessary”** in such cases. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - **“not undertake a transaction or commence an account-based relationship without following the CDD procedure.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - **“specify the mandatory information to be sought for KYC purposes while opening an account and during the periodic updation.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - Where additional information is needed but not specified in policy, **“obtain additional information … with the explicit consent of the customer.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - Apply CDD **“at the UCIC level”** so that for an existing KYC‑compliant customer opening another account/product, **“there shall be no need for a fresh CDD exercise as far as identification of the customer is concerned.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - CDD for **all joint holders**. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - **“clearly spell out the circumstances”** when a customer can act on behalf of another person/entity. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - Have a system to ensure customer identity does not match sanctions lists under Chapter IX: **“put in place a suitable system to ensure that the identity of the customer does not match with any person or entity, whose name appears in the sanctions lists…”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - If PAN is obtained: **“verify the Permanent Account Number (PAN) … from the verification facility of the issuing authority.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - If equivalent e‑document is obtained: **“verify the customer’s digital signature on the equivalent e-document … as per … the Information Technology Act, 2000.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - If GST details are available: **“verify the … GST number from the search / verification facility of the issuing authority.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
- CAP must not be exclusionary: it **“shall not result in denial of a banking / financial facility to members of the general public, especially those who are financially or socially disadvantaged, including the Persons with Disabilities (PwDs).”** Also, the bank **“shall not reject an application … without application of mind”** and must record reasons for rejection. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
- If suspicion of ML/TF and performing CDD would tip‑off: **“it shall not pursue the CDD process, and instead file an STR with FIU-IND.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]

**Operating procedures (what your SOPs should operationalise)**
- Account opening acceptance/rejection workflow that:
  - blocks anonymous/fictitious/benami accounts [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - prevents starting relationship/transactions before CDD completion [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - enforces UCIC‑level reuse for existing KYC‑compliant customers [B. Other General General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - enforces joint‑holder CDD [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - ensures reasoned rejections and recorded reasons [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
- “Acting on behalf of” controls (POA/authorised signatory/beneficial owner) aligned to required CDD capture for individuals acting as beneficial owners/authorised signatories/POA holders. [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals), p.25]

**Systems (technology/control expectations explicit or implied by Directions)**
- Sanctions name‑screening system covering onboarding. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
- PAN verification integration / workflow (where obtained). [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
- Digital signature verification workflow for equivalent e‑documents. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
- GST verification workflow where GST is available. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]

**MI (Board/Senior Management oversight indicators supported by Directions)**
- Metrics/exception reporting for:
  - number and reasons of onboarding/KYC updation rejections (since reasons must be recorded). [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - CAP/C DD failures and STRs filed where CDD could not be completed (CAP expects STR consideration). [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
  - sanctions screening matches and disposition. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]

---

### B) **Risk Management**

**Regulatory requirements that must be embedded**
- For risk management, the bank must have **“a risk-based approach”** including:
  - categorising customers **“into low, medium, and high-risk categories”** [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22]
  - risk categorisation parameters including **identity**, **social/financial status**, **nature of business**, **geographical risk**, **products/services**, **delivery channel**, and transaction types (cash/cheque/wire transfers/forex etc.). [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22]
  - confidentiality: **“shall keep the risk categorisation … and the specific reasons … confidential”** to avoid tipping off. [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22]
- The bank **“shall carry out ‘Money Laundering (ML) and Terrorist Financing (TF) Risk Assessment’ exercises periodically”**, document them, and **“review it at least annually.”** The periodicity is determined by the Board/Board committee, and the **outcome must be presented to the Board/committee.** [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]
- Bank must apply an RBA and have **“Board-approved policies, controls and procedures”**, implement a CDD programme commensurate with risk and size, and **“monitor the implementation of the controls and enhance them if necessary.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]

**Operating procedures**
- Customer risk rating methodology incorporating the required parameters (delivery channel, geography, product, transaction type). [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22]
- Periodic ML/TF risk assessment lifecycle: conduct, document, annual review, and Board reporting. [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]
- Confidential handling rules so frontline staff do not reveal risk categories/reasons. [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22]

**Systems**
- Risk scoring/rating engine capturing required risk inputs (customer identity profile, geography, product/delivery channel, transaction types). [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22]
- Repository/workflow to document ML/TF Risk Assessment and evidence annual review and Board presentation. [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]

**MI**
- Board/committee MI pack evidencing:
  - documented ML/TF risk assessment and **annual review** [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]
  - population by risk category (low/medium/high) and key risk drivers (kept confidential externally). [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22]

---

### C) **Customer Identification Procedures (CIP)**

**Regulatory triggers when CIP must be undertaken**
- The bank must identify customers in the cases listed in paragraph 21, including:
  - **“Commencement of an account-based relationship”** [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]
  - **international money transfer** for a non‑account holder [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]
  - doubts about authenticity/adequacy of identification data [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]
  - **selling third‑party products / own products / payment of credit card dues / prepaid or travel cards / any other product for more than ₹50,000** [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]
  - walk‑in customer transactions **“equal to or exceeds ₹50,000”** (single or connected) [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]
  - structuring below threshold where bank has reason to believe intentional structuring [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]
  - **“ensure it does not seek introductions while opening accounts.”** [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]

**CDD inputs for individuals at onboarding (CIP/C DD linkage)**
- For CDD of individuals, the bank must obtain (among others):
  - Aadhaar number in specified circumstances / voluntary submission options, or proof of possession of Aadhaar / OVD / equivalent e‑document, or KYC Identifier with consent to download from CKYCR; and **PAN or equivalent e‑document or Form 60**. [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals), p.25]

**Recognised onboarding/verification channels and key procedural controls**
- **Digital KYC process** requirements include an authenticated application, controlled access, live photograph, and watermarking with CAF number, GPS coordinates, officer identity, date/time. [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals) – Digital KYC Process, p.27]
- **V‑CIP definition and regulatory classification:** V‑CIP is defined as an alternative method involving secure live audio‑visual interaction and audit trail; and critically, **“The bank shall treat such processes complying with prescribed standards and procedures on par with face-to-face CIP for the purpose of this Direction.”** [C. Definitions, p.13]
- **V‑CIP standards** include infrastructure, encryption, IP controls, geo‑tagging/time stamping, liveness/spoof detection, trained officials, workflow/SOP requirement, and making accounts operational only after **concurrent audit**. [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals) – V-CIP, pp.31-35]
- **Third‑party reliance for CDD** is permitted subject to conditions such as obtaining records immediately, ensuring availability on request, regulated/supervised third party with CDD and record‑keeping measures, third party not in high‑risk jurisdiction, and **ultimate responsibility remains with the bank**. [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]
- **CKYCR uploading**: the bank must **“capture customer’s KYC records and upload onto CKYCR within 10 days of commencement of an account-based relationship.”** [Chapter X – Other Instructions, p.61]

**Operating procedures**
- CIP trigger checklist embedded in:
  - account opening, international remittance for non‑customers, and walk‑in ₹50,000+ scenarios. [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]
- Document collection/verification flows consistent with the individual CDD menu (Aadhaar/proof/OVD/e‑doc/KYC Identifier, PAN/Form 60). [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals), p.25]
- Digital KYC SOP aligned to live photo + watermark requirements. [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals) – Digital KYC Process, p.27]
- V‑CIP SOP and workflow (explicitly mandated): **“Each bank shall formulate a clear workflow and standard operating procedure for V-CIP and ensure adherence to it.”** [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals) – V-CIP, p.32]
- Third‑party reliance SOP (immediate receipt of CDD records, high‑risk jurisdiction checks, accountability). [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]
- CKYCR upload SOP meeting the **10‑day** timeline. [Chapter X – Other Instructions, p.61]

**Systems**
- Digital KYC application with access controls and required watermarking/GPS stamping. [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals) – Digital KYC Process, p.27]
- V‑CIP platform meeting encryption, geo‑tagging, liveness detection, audit trail, India IP restrictions, secure storage in India, and concurrent audit gating for operability. [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals) – V-CIP, pp.31-35]
- CKYCR integration to upload within 10 days and retrieve using KYC Identifier (and prevent unnecessary re‑submission where CKYCR record is usable). [Chapter X – Other Instructions, pp.61-62]

**MI**
- CIP/KYC completion dashboards:
  - onboarding volumes by method (Digital KYC / V‑CIP / other permitted modes as used by the bank), and concurrent audit pass/fail for V‑CIP opened accounts. [Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals) – V-CIP, pp.31-35]
  - CKYCR uploads within **10 days** compliance rate. [Chapter X – Other Instructions, p.61]
  - third‑party reliance instances and confirmation that records were obtained immediately. [Chapter V – CIP, pp.23-24]

---

### D) **Monitoring of Transactions (Ongoing due diligence + alerts + reporting)**

**Regulatory monitoring requirements**
- The bank **“shall undertake ongoing due diligence”** to ensure transactions are consistent with knowledge of the customer, business, risk profile, and source of funds/wealth. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
- The bank must **necessarily monitor** transaction types including:
  - **“Large and complex transactions including RTGS transactions”** and unusual patterns without rationale [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
  - transactions exceeding thresholds for specific categories of accounts [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
  - high turnover inconsistent with balance [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
  - third‑party instruments deposited followed by large cash withdrawals. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
- Monitoring must be risk‑aligned: **“align the extent of monitoring with the risk category of the customer.”** [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
- Periodic review of risk categorisation system: **“at least once in every six months”** and establish need for enhanced due diligence. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
- The bank may consider AI/ML innovations to support monitoring. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
- FIU reporting enablement: to identify and report suspicious transactions effectively, the bank must implement **“robust software that generates alerts when transactions are inconsistent with a customer's risk categorisation and updated profile.”** [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to FIU‑IND, pp.54-55]
- For third‑party products, AML software is required: **“AML software capable of capturing, generating and analysing alerts for the purpose of filing CTR / STR … shall be available.”** [Chapter X – Other Instructions, pp.71-71]
- STR confidentiality and operational constraint: **“The bank shall not put any restriction on operations in the accounts merely on the basis of the STR filed.”** [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to FIU‑IND, pp.54-55]

**Operating procedures**
- Scenario‑based monitoring SOP covering the mandatory monitored typologies (complex/unusual/threshold‑exceeding/high turnover/third‑party instruments + cash withdrawals). [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
- Risk‑based escalation and EDD triggers aligned to six‑monthly risk category review. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]
- FIU reporting SOP aligned to use of FIU formats/utilities where required and confidentiality controls (no tipping‑off). [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to FIU‑IND, pp.54-55]

**Systems**
- AML monitoring system generating alerts inconsistent with risk category and updated profile (explicit). [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to FIU‑IND, pp.54-55]
- AML monitoring capability for third‑party product transactions (explicit). [Chapter X – Other Instructions, p.71]

**MI**
- Monitoring effectiveness MI:
  - alert volumes by risk category and typology; time‑to‑dispose; STR/CTR generation statistics (driven by “robust software” expectation). [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to FIU‑IND, pp.54-55]
  - evidence of six‑monthly risk review completion. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]

---

## 3) Governance, control assurance, and accountability that must support all 4 elements
- Compliance with KYC policy must include allocation of responsibility, independent evaluation, concurrent/internal audit, and **“submission of quarterly audit notes and compliance to the Audit Committee.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
- The bank **“shall ensure that it does not outsource the decision-making functions of determining compliance with KYC norms.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]
- Principal Officer responsibilities include **“ensuring compliance, monitoring transactions, and sharing and reporting information”**; and designated director must be Board‑nominated and communicated to FIU‑IND and RBI (and cannot be the Principal Officer). [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21]

---

## 4) Practical deliverable format you can use (RBI‑aligned)
To satisfy the Directions using a single Board‑approved KYC Policy pack (and supporting Board‑approved sub‑policies), structure your documentation and MI evidence as:
- **KYC Policy (Board approved):** explicitly organised into 4 chapters (CAP / Risk Management / CIP / Transaction Monitoring) with an annex for periodic updation, exceptional updation measures, OVD for change of address, and “any branch” updation facility. [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]
- **Board‑approved sub‑policies:** mobile number change DD for non‑face‑to‑face opened accounts; correspondent banking parameters + approval governance. [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17]
- **Systems & MI evidence folder:** quarterly audit notes to Audit Committee; annual ML/TF risk assessment outcomes to Board; V‑CIP concurrent audit logs; CKYCR upload TAT and exceptions; AML alerts/STR workflow evidence. [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17; B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21; Chapter VI – CDD Procedure (Individuals) – V-CIP, pp.31-35; Chapter X – Other Instructions, p.61; Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to FIU‑IND, pp.54-55]
